query: distinguishing features of Crohn’s disease versus ulcerative colitis

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Differentiation from Crohn's Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal TB and Crohn's disease are granulomatous diseases with similar clinical, radiological, endoscopic, and histologic features. [62] Distinguishing between these conditions can be difficult, and misdiagnoses have been reported. [63] Both TB skin tests and IGRAs are of limited value in helping to establish a diagnosis. Numerous studies have attempted to develop criteria to help distinguish between intestinal TB and Crohn's disease with limited success. [21] While there can be differences in endoscopic and histologic morphology between TB and Crohn's disease lesions, much overlap exists.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease: can manifest with bowel inflammation and strictures resembling malignancy

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses to consider when assessing patients with possible Crohn disease are listed below. Documentation of travel and exposure history and judicious use of laboratory, imaging, and histologic tests help differentiate the above diagnoses. Infectious enteritis and terminal ileitis Coccidioides Histoplasma Salmonella Tuberculosis Yersinia Infectious colitis Amebiasis Campylobacter C.difficile Cytomegalovirus E.coli Salmonella Shigella Noninfectious Behcet disease Common variable immunodeficiency Diverticulitis Drug-induced colitis, eg, from nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and immunotherapy Ischemic colitis Sarcoidosis Segmental colitis associated with diverticulosis Small vessel vasculitis Solitary rectal ulcer syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ulcerative colitis: symptoms may overlap with those of malignancy

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: UC and CD are differentiated histologically by the depth of involvement in the bowel wall. Both forms of IBD can develop with extraintestinal manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: Colitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Multidetector CT and thin sections, can accurately demonstrate inflammatory changes in the colonic wall and help assess the extent of disease. Ulcerative colitis is distinguishable from granulomatous colitis (Crohn disease) in terms of location of involvement, extent, and appearance of colonic wall thickening, and type of complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Celiac Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory bowel disease, including Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis, can resemble celiac disease with symptoms such as abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, and weight loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Colitis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for infectious colitis include the following: Inflammatory bowel disease Colorectal cancer Diverticulitis Ischemic colitis Irritable bowel disease Drug-induced colitis Radiation-associated colitis Colitis complicating immune deficiency disorders Graft-versus-host disease Acute appendicitis or ileocecal mass; the differential diagnosis may include: Yersinia enterocolitica infection Crohn disease Amoebic colitis Ulcerative colitis Colorectal cancer involving the cecum

retrieved_docs.title: Amebic Colitis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses include appendicitis, IBD, pseudomembranous colitis, and tuberculosis. Rarely, E histolytica can affect the appendix, representing an extension of an infection of the right colon. Typical IBD does not show fibrinous materials containing organisms. Ulcerative colitis characteristically shows more diffuse colonic involvement with mucosal distortion and lamina propria basal plasma cell infiltration. Crohn's disease may show patchy mucosal ulceration but is distinguishable by fissure-like ulcers rather than flask-shaped ulceration; ulcers from Crohn's disease are mostly horizontal, whereas the amebic ulcers grow perpendicular to the long axis of the bowel. [18] [19] In colon tuberculosis, grossly, ulceration with diffuse fibrosis extends through the wall, causing stenosis and obstruction. Coexistent tuberculous peritonitis presents in rare cases. Microscopically, typical granulomas are usually present. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Celiac disease can have a similar symptom profile to chronic diarrhea. Celiac disease should be tested for by serologic testing. If necessary, this can also be tested for by small bowel biopsy, which shows flattening of villi. Crohn disease is an inflammatory bowel disease that typically causes diarrhea. This particular disease typically presents with patchy colitis and various other characteristics, such as perianal disease, like fistulas or fissures. On biopsy, granulomas are generally present along with transmural inflammation. Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), which is diarrhea-predominant, is also a consideration. IBS is typically considered a diagnosis of exclusion. Typically, symptoms of IBS include not only diarrhea but crampy abdominal pain that is improved by defecation and frequent changes in the consistency and frequency of bowel movements.

retrieved_docs.title: Cytomegalovirus Colitis (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis for cytomegalovirus colitis includes: Viral/bacterial gastroenteritis Inflammatory bowel disease Colorectal cancer Toxic megacolon Diverticulitis Irritable bowel disease Celiac disease Graft-versus-host disease

retrieved_docs.title: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Histologic changes define the 2 types of microscopic colitis. This presentation of symptoms often leads to evaluation for other types of inflammatory bowel disease, including Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. The incidence has been increasing throughout northern Europe and northern North America, which is more common in women. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation will usually reveal an increase in inflammatory factors (ESR, CRP, leukocytosis), especially during an acute flare. Regardless of disease stage, 60% to 70% of ulcerative colitis patients are positive for perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (P-ANCA). P-ANCA is also found in a small number of patients with Crohn disease. In addition to P-ANCA, anti-saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies (ASCA) are found in both Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis but are more prevalent in Crohn disease; therefore, testing for both P-ANCA and ASCA has some utility in distinguishing types. Testing for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) can also be helpful in ulcerative colitis, as higher levels can indicate a flare.

retrieved_docs.title: Pericholangitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The male predominance observed in large duct pathology was confirmed in the small duct patients with inflammatory bowel disease, although not in the group of patients without IBD, where females predominated. [9] A study by Bjornsson et al. on 83 patients with small-duct PSC showed 51 males (61.5%) and 32 females (38.5%). This study reported the median age at the time of small duct PSC diagnosis was 38 years. It reported no significant ethnicity consistency. [10] In a study by Singal et al. on 25 patients with SDPSC, the mean age of diagnosing was 37 years old, with a male to female ratio of 13:12. [11] The study by Charatcharoenwitthaya et al. on forty-two patients with SDPSC was confirmed the male dominance. [12] Heikius et al., in a Finnish study, evaluated the prevalence of SDPSC in IBD patients. After studying 237 unselected patients with IBD, they reported the prevalence of PSC was 11%. Of these patients, 11.5% had the small-duct variant. [13] Among adults with PSC, 75% have involvement of both small and large ducts, 15% small ducts only, and 10% large ducts only. Small duct PSC typically presents with an insidious onset of symptoms or with end-stage liver disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Pericholangitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Charatcharoenwitthaya et al. reported that patients with concomitant of SDPSC and IBD have a better prognosis compare to SDPSC without IBD. Also, they concluded IBD associated with SDPSC had a lower prevalence of pancolitis compared to IBD in patients with large-duct PSC. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease is slightly more common in females compared to males, but ulcerative colitis appears to be equally present in both genders. IBD is generally a disorder in developed countries and colder climates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the sulfasalazine compound, indicating that these 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives constitute the active moiety released from sulfasalazine after colonic bacterial metabolism. Sulfasalazine, a combination of sulfapyridine and 5‑aminosalicylic acid, is employed in the treatment of ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, and its therapeutic effect relies on the release of aminosalicylic acid and related agents within the intestine. Recognizing aminosalicylic acid and its analogues as integral components of sulfasalazine helps clinicians understand the drug’s mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1056
retrieved_docs.content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with both الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray) and the gastrointestinal disorder gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis is situated within a disease hierarchy, being a specific manifestation of الِتهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis) and serving as the parent condition for الِتهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, a more localized ulcerative form of gastritis. Consequently, the identification of SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX may lead clinicians to evaluate thoracic imaging findings together with assessment for gastritis and duodenitis and its related subtypes, supporting an integrated approach to diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.

Diverticulosis and diverticulitis are classified under “رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ” (colonic diverticulum). Both “انْثِقاب القَرْهَة المَعِدِيّة” (intestinal ulcer excavation) and “رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ” (colonic diverticulum) serve as parent categories of the broader disorder “اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ” (colonic disorder). The broader colonic disorder, in turn, has a wider relationship with “anal and rectal conditions”, indicating that disorders of the colon, such as diverticulosis, diverticulitis, and intestinal ulcerations, are clinically connected to conditions affecting the anal and rectal regions. Overall, the knowledge network links bleeding‑related terminology to both anemia and gastrointestinal lesions, while grouping colonic diverticular disease and intestinal ulceration under a common colonic disorder that relates to anal and rectal conditions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with findings on الأَشِعَّة السِّينِيَّة على الصَّدْر (chest X‑ray) as well as with gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis, in turn, are clinically associated with a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (positive HIV test), indicating that these upper gastrointestinal inflammatory conditions may occur more frequently in patients with HIV infection. Within the hierarchical classification of gastrointestinal disorders, gastritis and duodenitis is a child of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis) and itself has a more specific child entity, الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (ulcerative or erosive gastritis). This network of relationships highlights how a complex of signs and symptoms can be linked to both imaging findings and specific inflammatory gastrointestinal diseases, which may be further influenced by underlying immunodeficiency such as HIV, and how these diseases are organized within broader and narrower diagnostic categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray) and also with gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis, in turn, includes الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ as a subordinate condition, indicating that the symptom complex may be linked both to radiographic chest findings and to inflammatory gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcer disease. This interconnectedness highlights the importance of evaluating both thoracic imaging and gastrointestinal pathology when assessing patients presenting with this sign‑symptom complex.

Diverticulosis and diverticulitis are grouped within the functional bowel disorder category (رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ), which together with “ulcer excavation” serves as a parent for the broader functional intestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). This functional intestinal disorder is therefore linked to both structural ulcerative processes and functional bowel rhythm abnormalities, illustrating its dual nature in gastrointestinal pathology. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are linked to a broader category of intestinal functional disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). This intestinal functional disorder is organized under two parent categories: ulcer excavation (انْثِقاب القَرْحَة المَعِدِيّة) and functional diverticulosis (رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ). Diverticulosis and diverticulitis are classified as types of functional diverticulosis, while gastroduodenal ulcer (except hemorrhage) is classified as a form of ulcer excavation. Additionally, duodenal ulcer (القَرْهَة المَعِدِيّة الاثْناعشَرِيّة) is also a child of the ulcer excavation category. These relationships illustrate how various gastrointestinal conditions are grouped within broader pathological frameworks, aiding clinicians in diagnosis and management.
